column7: message

> 3) Automation is coming regardless of a minimum wage. Even in the US in some of the lowest wage areas, McDonalds put their automation machines. If it's doable, corporations will do it.

I agree with this, but have to add that an increased minimum wage has the potential to make automation cost-effective (say, if it costs "$12/hr" to automate, and we are talking about a mw of $10 vs $15).

As you said, McD's decision was likely not affected by the (low) minimum wage, but I could see future situations where business owners do this math.  Hell, even if automation is not currently cost-effective, I could see a business investing the $$ because they see the 'writing on the wall' that the mw will increase.

1) Some US States have a minimum wage of approximately 11$ per hour, equal to our 15

2)Product prices do not increase in a 1:1 ratio with labor costs. Basic necessities might go up 10%, but people have 33% more money to spend

3) Automation is coming regardless of a minimum wage. Even in the US in some of the lowest wage areas, McDonalds put their automation machines. If it's doable, corporations will do it.

The biggest issues I see with this is the effects it wall have on rural Ontario. While 15$/hour is simply not enough to live in Toronto, it is a great wage for rural land where mortgages are 200k. They will really feel the negatives

@rosy-brandy, you make a good point about the potential for increased minimum wage to drive automation, but @interior-flag brings up a valid concern about rural areas. Maybe we should be talking about a regional minimum wage instead of a blanket $15 across the province?

That's an interesting discussion. @square-scale, your suggestion about a regional minimum wage is worth exploring. @interior-flag, can you elaborate on how a higher minimum wage might affect rural areas specifically, and what kind of challenges do you think a regional minimum wage could help address?

- If not: output "".

- **Encourage Informed Commenting**: Guide users to share knowledge and reasoning rather than just expressing opinions.

- **Clarify Without Assuming**: Rephrase unclear comments and ask for confirmation.  
- **Be Welcoming**: Make participants feel valued and part of the community.

- **Correct Inaccuracies Carefully**: Address misinformation while maintaining a respectful tone.

column7: "@moderator, I think @interior-flag has a point about rural areas, but let's not forget that a higher minimum wage can also bring in more tax revenue for those areas. Maybe instead of a regional minimum wage, we could look at other ways to support rural businesses, like tax breaks or subsidies? @square-scale, don't you think a regional minimum wage could get pretty complicated to implement?"

That's a great point, @rosy-brandy. @square-scale, how do you think the potential complexity of implementing a regional minimum wage could be balanced with the potential benefits it might bring to rural areas?

"@generative-points, let's focus on discussing the ideas rather than making assumptions about each other's intentions. @square-scale, would you like to respond to @generative-points' suggestion that tax breaks or subsidies might be a more straightforward solution?"

column7:
> If you change it, and you don't acknowledge/respect the roots of the cultural icon you're changing, then you are appropriating it. It's very simple. 

That contradicts the dictionary definition you laid out in the previous paragraph.

> Why do you have to promote Hindu culture (accurately)?

You don't *have* to. But if you're going to invoke a culture, and you don't respect/accurately portray the roots of that culture, then you're appropriating that culture. 

>And you just like something without it being accurate?

Yes, you can like whatever you want. The term "appropriate" means to "repurpose for personal gain." Wearing a bindi in a music video is far different than "liking" bindis. Liking isn't a self-serving act. 

>Can't you change something (like Elvis perhaps did) and make it your own?

Sure. If you change it, and you don't acknowledge/respect the roots of the cultural icon you're changing, then you are appropriating it. It's very simple.

@chalky-euphemism, @rosy-brandy, you're both missing the point. If I like something from another culture, I shouldn't need a history lesson to wear it or use it. It's just a matter of personal taste, not some deep cultural statement.
"@interior-flag, that's an interesting perspective. Can you help us understand what you mean by 'personal taste' in this context, and how you think that differs from cultural appropriation?"

column7: Honestly think having the “doesn’t matter” option on this poll didn’t really help with getting which one is preferred . I would also choose doesn’t matter cause it’s saying the same thing. But if had to choose between the two, I would say black. Also the poll is 10 years old. Views have changed.

> Most black people call themselves African American

[This poll of black Americans](http://news.gallup.com/poll/28816/black-african-american.aspx) shows that more people prefer the term "African American" than prefer the term "black".  However, more importantly, in my opinion, it shows that a majority don't care. Unfortunately, I can't find a poll showing how black Americans refer to themselves.

@flat-buffer, that poll is even older than the one we're discussing, doesn't really prove anything about current preferences. And @square-scale, I kinda agree with you, the "doesn't matter" option is pretty vague, but I still think it's better than nothing. What's the point of even having a poll if we're just gonna say it doesn't matter anyway?

"Let's focus on the discussion at hand. @interior-flag, you raise a valid point about the age of the polls. @square-scale and @flat-buffer, your insights on the 'doesn't matter' option and the terminology preferences are appreciated. To move forward, can you all share what you think would be a more effective way to gauge current preferences on this topic?"

column7:
>“Better” in what sense?   

  A child can convey just as much feeling in a picture of her daddy, as that black paint, but without the prior knowledge of loss. You don't need the sob story to "get it".  
  The kids pic will stand alone. It will have an arrangement of idols, a set of skill involved. It will have layers.  
  This painting has none of these things.
  

>That said, what wouldn't happen is for “a kid” to come up with *the idea of making such a painting*.

  I disagree, my kids regularly do "design" pages, a pattern. Just something to look nice.  

>rare to see a painting in a gallery with large areas of bare canvas, so that would attract the attention.  
 
  It's still only promoting thought, not admiration of a painting. Public will be asking: "why is that here, wtf? Is this wallpaper?"  They will not be saying, "hmm,  nice painting"

  >There's some questions to ponder here about positive and negative space, and ground vs figure. 

You can apply that thought to any piece of art ever. That doesn't make this piece special. 

>And this painting is *enormous*, almost three metres high and more than two wide. I've never seen it but I can imagine that it is a very imposing piece, it will dominate even quite a large exhibition space.  
 
  It's big. Ok, but again, the piece of art is not selling itself.  You need prior knowledge or you need to ask universal, open ended questions to even begin appreciating it.  


>, they might have to be told that this life event is what the piece refers to, sure, but once you do know that, it begins to make sense, can you really not see that?
  

I can see where the artist was coming from, kind of, it's very subjective. But again, i could give a sob story before any picture,  ever, and we could all say, "you don't see it"?  
  "And everyone will say , "Yeah,  kind of...."  
  But nobody will actually see the same thing. And we'll all be none the wiser. It's in the beholder, again the art piece has done nothing, the provoking idea is the story.   
   It's black paint.  
  
  And all that means this piece of art is useless in this context. To appreciate it you need a back story,  well then, why doesnt every single art piece ever, get a back story?  
   But why do we only need the back story for this piece? Why is this exceptional?  It only shows the artists inept attempt to convey anything at all.


>Is this painting “worth” eighty-five million dollars? 

I'm not worried about its value, my daughters pictures are priceless to me, the value is what you pay, but I'm not going to impress my kids pictures on the public by exhibiting them, and give my back story, so the public will understand,  Increasing the knowledge about the pictures won't add artistic value to the stand alone piece.

>Is that wrong or bad?  

  If you like it, good for you, but as a whole, it's bad,  people will look at this example of a great 20th century art movement and think, was he a lazy artist?

> It is a crap painting. A kid, with little effort, really could do better. 

“Better” in what sense? That's a genuine open question—what would make a kid's painting more meritorious than this? Is it the technical execution that you object to? The restricted palette? What?

That said, what wouldn't happen is for “a kid” to come up with *the idea of making such a painting*.

> I bet most would walk by it if it was in a gallery.

No, I don't think they would. For one thing, it's rare to see a painting in a gallery with large areas of bare canvas, so that would attract the attention. There's some questions to ponder here about positive and negative space, and ground vs figure. 

And this painting is *enormous*, almost three metres high and more than two wide. I've never seen it but I can imagine that it is a very imposing piece, it will dominate even quite a large exhibition space.

> Certainly, you wouldn't stop to analyse the loss of Barnett's younger brother in the single mono colour on white.

The kind of people who go to galleries to see abstract art probably would. Now, they might have to be told that this life event is what the piece refers to, sure, but once you do know that, it begins to make sense. The piece very clearly has something to say about separation, about transition, about presence and absence, about sadness…can you really not see that?

> is it a terrible piece of art, or am I an ignoramus?

Those are not the only two options.

>Christies explaining the piece […] use a thesaurus to help bring it to life, reading it is like reading a child filling up his 1000 word project

Yes, that sales blather is pretty terrible. 

Is this painting “worth” eighty-five million dollars? In one sense, yes—that is its clearly established market value. Does a great deal of that value arise from the history and web of associations that the painting fits into? Certainly. Is that wrong or bad? I don't think so. If you do: why do you?

@rosy-brandy, I see you're drinking the art world's Kool-Aid. Just because something is huge and has a "deep" backstory doesn't mean it's good art. I mean, I could paint a giant white canvas with a tiny black dot in the middle and call it "Existential Crisis" and you'd probably eat it up. @nuclear-hardball is right, a kid could do better than this. It's just a lazy attempt at being profound.

"@tomato-key, @rosy-brandy, and @nuclear-hardball, let's keep the discussion focused on the artwork and its meaning, rather than making personal comments. What are your thoughts on how the use of negative space in this painting contributes to its overall impact?"

column7: "@nuclear-hardball, oh please, do go on about how a kid could do better. I'm sure your extensive art background and expertise would be simply fascinating to hear.
